Hi Everyone -
I thought I would contribute to the collective knowledge here with my journey busting using Rivea Corymbosa Seeds, AKA "RC Seeds", AKA Ololiuqui. I will keep a log of it all in the format I've shown below. First I will give some quick background. (To skip to just the busting log, scroll to the bottom).
I'm a 35 year old male, first started getting CH when I was sixteen, in 2001. Cycles are generally ~7 weeks, and have been getting further and further apart the older I get, with ~3 years between cycles. When I was a teen I did the pharma route, finding mixed results with Prednisone taper and Inderal. But once those stopped working, a neurologist with a bad attitude started trying other drugs on me, and I started reading about the experiences of other CHers on clusterheadaches.com and here on clusterbusters, I said "no thank you" to the pharma route.
2003 - 2010
Sloppily experimenting with busting, different vitamins, prednisone/Inderal etc. I was 18-25 during this time, and you know how people are at that age! I had mixed results with mushrooms, and also had very mixed results with the high! One of the most enjoyable nights in my entire life was on mushrooms, and it stopped the CH dead in its tracks. I also had some of the most intensely anxious times in my life, and it didn't stop the CH. I attempted RC Seeds during this time, but back then the recommended starting dose was very low. I think I started with 8 seeds! So, I found no impact from using them.
2010 - 2020
Started the Vitamin D3 regimen. The D3 brought the severity of the pain during attacks WAY down. I've never been able to fully control a cycle using just the D3 regimen, but it has helped tremendously. During this time I also started using oxygen - *properly*! I was prescribed oxygen as a teenager, but was using the wrong mask and didn't have enough air flow, so I concluded that it didn't work for me. This was a total game-changer, obviously. I also started realizing that when I eat, I get attacks. I started talking to Batch a lot during this time. We talked about the body's PH and how keeping the body more alkaline could help. So I started drinking more lemon water, making tweaks like that, that I think helped a little bit. I tried 5-MEO-DALT for two cycles during this time as well. The results were mixed and I did not like the idea of weighing out these tiny, tiny increments of a random chemical I purchased in a sketchy way on the internet and ingesting it.
Latest Cycle - Started Nov. 2020
This cycle has been different for a number of reasons: 1) It came faster than expected. Typically the pain free time between my cycles increases by 3 months each cycle. It is crazy how accurately it has followed it my entire life! 3 months between my first cycle and my second, then six months, then 9 months, etc. etc. I was all the way up to three years between cycles, and then this latest one came after 2.5 years. This was very disappointing for me. 2) It has lasted much longer than

This is a collection of reports from DMT users who have posted here at ClusterBusters, showing a range of reactions to DMT and including some advice about using DMT.
It should be noted that from all the current evidence, DMT
does not
block busting.
“DMT works for me, at least for a mild attack, 3-4. Didn’t take much, and I’m typing this less than 5 minutes after taking it. At the dose I took it does feel faintly like a low dose of MM, not the same, but not strange.”
“…instead of immediately getting out of bed and heading for the O2, I just kept laying in bed for a minute or so and let it ramp up a bit, grabbed the vape and took 4 small tokes about 10 seconds apart.....and the hit just freakin dissolved in less than a minute and I immediately fell back asleep......2 hours later duplicated these results with my last hit of the nite!!
And the kicker is that I felt hardly anything in the way of “tripping”!!”
“DMT was the most effective for being able to immediately abort a headache in around 10 minutes. Because DMT is smoked and not eaten it has a much faster activation time and literally saved my life. For the last 13 years every time I have a cluster headache attack I smoke DMT out of a vaporizer pen and the relief is almost immediate.”
“Sourced DMT, used it last night to abort, it’s GREAT you feel the pain being sucked away, it only took 3 pulls on vape….
Saw a few strange things. I felt really warm inside, effects only lasted for around 10 mins, you can feel the pain getting sucked away, it’s unreal ”
“For me it hit kind of heavy early on and I thought maybe it was not sustainable to just keep ripping DMT all the time but later it got lighter on the brain and it seemed doable but I would def set a limit, like 1 pen (mine were .7G) a month with a month off or something like that for a chronic CH sufferer. You are going to love it and it becomes way too handy of a way to abort hits so IMO you should keep in mind that other abort methods should be mixed in to throttle the use of the DMT pen.”
“The DMT did not really bring much anxiety on and it goes away so fast that it’s not a problem. The high and abort set in within seconds depending on how deep you are into the attack. A stubborn attack may take an extra hit or two from the vape and take a minute or two to work.”
“I had a proper attack. I pulled out the DMT vape Ive kept in my bedside table, loaded it with wax and had a small amount (as Im not experienced with DMT I was worried about having too much and tripping while in pain). Didnt really feel anything and the cluster persisted. About a minute later I had a larger inhale and this time held. A few seconds after exhaling my fingers and toes started to tingle and the headache pain halved in about 10 seconds. After a minute without any serious tripping I had a 3rd inhale of about the same. The ceiling swam a bit and I saw some fractals and just like that the cluster was gone. Literally in a matter of seconds. 30 minutes later I was driving to work

Last night, completed Bust 4 with a 1/2 tab of Vitamin L.  Have not had a legit attack since before Bust 3 but still experience soreness and occasional shadows, so I thought one more Bust would be appropriate.
On the Bust itself:
- significant body discomfort, experienced mostly as jaw and neck tension combined with agitation.  This was not a serious problem, but it was not especially pleasant either.  I paced quite a bit
- lots of visual changes in the form of shifting patterns and breathing floors, walls, etc
All in all,
though I have certainly had some good times, I am
ready for a break from The Vitamin L recreational experience.
medicinal effects:
- during the Bust, experienced occasional pressure in CH area.  This was not significant enough to qualify as a shadow.
That's about it!
The night before last, I went out to a nice dinner with my SO and another couple we've had planned for months.  We had a wine pairing with our dinner, so I had seven different wines, mostly reds, and a sweet wine with dessert.  I mention it because red wine is an almost guaranteed trigger for me as is anything overly sweet.  The meal passed without even a shadow!  I even had a couple martinis at cocktail hour before dinner.  I had an injection of Imitrex back in my room in case things broke bad, but did not have to think about it.
normally, I wouldn't dream of drinking so much as a beer during or around a CH cycle but with the success of the Vitamin L and D, I was able to enjoy myself.  It's a different world.  We've spent the past month wondering if we should cancel this dinner because of my cycle; quite empowering to go through with it like this.
*****
if anybody is reading this looking for advice:
-start the MM or L as soon as you can, and space them apart 5 days, per protocol.  I did 4 to avoid a Christmas Day Bust but I think 5 would have been better
-start the Vitamin D regime immediately. I eventually doubled my D to 20,000 ius a day but YMMV.  I'm a big guy.
-try aborting attacks with ice cold water and 5 hour energy drinks (these are far superior to any others, because you can bolt them down immediately).
-very strong ginger for shadows and neck massages at cluster spots, specifically, tendons on side of neck and under ear.  Strong coffee helped my shadows as well
-only take Imitrex if it's an emergency.  I have found I get significant increase in cluster attacks following Imitrex use and a marked increase in pain level
-keep a diary and be methodical.   When did your cycle start?  When does each attack start? How long does an attack last?  What did you do to stop it?  Use this to identify possible triggers and possible actions that help you.   It's also something to focus on and be constructive with when you're up in the middle of the night in excruciating pain
-find a doctor who will prescribe oxygen for you. It is not expensive out of pocket or through insurance.  Advocate for yourself on this or ask someone to help you advocate.

When I arrived at clusterbusters in October of 2009 I had been an episodic clusterhead for 25 years. My cycles were unpredictable the first few years but had settled into a pattern by 1995.....5 to 6 month cycle (all beginning between late July and mid August), followed by a 18 to 19 month remission with all of the cycles during that time period occurring in odd numbered years.....anywhere from 3 to 8 hits daily, no completely painfree days, average O2 abort times of approximately 15 to 20 minutes.
By the time that I had acquired busting meds in December of 2009 my cycle had ended so I adopted an every 90 day maintenance dosing routine in an attempt to prevent my 2011 cycle. However, my 2011 began as scheduled in early August.....dosed every 5 to 7 days for that entire 20 week cycle to achieve 2 to 4 totally painfree days between doses. Oxygen abort times improved and the majority of hits were dispatched in 10 to 15 minutes.
My 2013 and 2015 cycles were pretty much an exact repeat of above but O2 abort times were down to 10 minutes for most hits.
In late February of 2017 I began what I termed a "preemptive busting" strategy by dosing every 30 days hoping to prevent my 2017 cycle....I followed that routine thru early October......and the Beast never raised his ugly head!! Not the first twinge!!
Went back to dosing every 90 days with a plan of resuming the preemptive strategy in February of 2019. However, the Beast put in a surprise appearance in February of 2018!! Immediately began dosing every 5 days and that breakthrough cycle only lasted for about 7 weeks.......same pattern of 2 totally painfree days between doses, and almost all of the hits were extremely mild with the vast majority aborted in 5 minutes or less!!! To put this in perspective, during my last full 20 week cycle in 2015 I went thru 1 and 3/4 large M sized tanks of welding oxygen......so, when the 2018 cycle started, I brought that 1/4 tank full in from the barn.........IT STILL HAS ENUF O2 TO ABORT SEVERAL HITS!!!!
I resumed monthly dosing in Feb of 2019 and again, no visits from the Beast!!
My wife and I talked it over and ultimately decided that a monthly "journey" from here on out was a small price to pay in an attempt to stifle the Bastard Beast forever!!
So, to recap, in the past 58 months I've had 1 very short and mild 7 week cycle!!!!  Yeee Feckin Hawww!!!
So much respect and gratitude to Flash, Pink Shark Mark, Bob Wold, and the other early busting pioneers for their efforts and perseverance in bringing this treatment avenue to clusterheads all over the world!!

Hello Cluster Family,
I just wanted to share my current experience with you all. First, a little back story, I have been a sufferer of clusters since I was 15 years old, I have seen so many different doctors and been given more pills than I could ever count. None of it helped. I had a cluster 1 week after giving birth to my youngest son, and all I remember was sitting on the bathroom floor, sleep deprived, in agony, legitimately wondering if I was going to lose my grip on reality and go insane.
Earlier this year I finally saw a neurologist that officially diagnosed me with CH, and I remember I immediately felt relief, because I had thought for years that this is what I had, but no doctor would diagnose me because the disorder is "primarily in men". It only took seconds for the relief to suddenly be taken over by despair. An incurable neurological disorder, that there is no known cause or cure. I spent several months in severe depression and anxiety, accompanied by insomnia from fear of sleep, as that is when my attack occur.
My clusters are normally triggered by extreme stress, or seasonal changes, so when summer started giving way to fall, my normal 6 week cluster period started. For 3 weeks I was going through 2 oxygen tanks a week, being woken up hourly from midnight to 7 am in excruciating pain. After being shamed into taking more prescription meds by my neurologist, I took verapamil for the first time. I took it at 5pm, and by 730 I was in the worst pain I have ever been in. I know this cluster was brought on by the medication because I do not normally get attacks in the evening. My clusters are very predictable, and always occur at night once I have gone to sleep. This attack very nearly put me in the hospital, and the only reason it didn't was because my husband gave me something that calmed me down enough to be able to withstand the pain.

https://vitamindwiki.com/Cluster+headaches+substantially+reduced+by+10,000+IU+of+Vitamin+D+in+80+percent+of+people
Unfortunately, I did not have all the items in the regimen in my medicine cabinet, but I live in the Pacific North West, and as we do get less sun here than in other parts of the county, so I had previously purchased a  vitamin D3 dropper with 5,000ui per serving, which I had not taken because I am habitually bad at remembering to take medication or vitamins. So I decided to start taking two doses in the morning. I figured it couldn't hurt.
Now remember, at this time I am at about the halfway point in my normal 6 week cluster period. I started taking the vitamin D3, and did not feel any different the first night. Hi everyone! I want to share my successful bust story and share what one should expect moving to or coming to Portugal with a CH.
A breif note on my case:
I have episodic CH
Last year, my doctor in Turkey prescribed me Cymbalta (Duloxetine, an SNRI) as an antidepressant. I am still taking 60 mg daily
I am a 6’6”, 200-pound male (which may or may not be relevant to the RC dose and side effects)
I moved to Portugal in early February, and my cycle started in mid-February
I hadn't had any busting experience before
CH treatment in Portugal:
You must have a prescription from a local doctor to get triptans. I assume a prescription from any doctor in Europe would be accepted, but mine was in Turkish and wasn’t accepted
In mid-February, the earliest available appointment with a neurologist in Porto or Braga was in mid-March. I went to a family doctor who gave me a prescription, but he could only prescribe a small number of pills, like high single digit. To get a sufficient supply for a full cycle, you need to see a neurologist
I was lucky enough to find an appointment in late February in a small town in northern Portugal. The doctor was quite knowledgeable and prescribed me an entire warchest of triptans. I think I now have enough for 2–3 bad cycles
Oxygen tanks are not easy to obtain here. You must be registered in the Portuguese healthcare system, and only a pulmonologist can prescribe them to you. The neurologist tried to get me prescription without me going to a pulmonologist, but ultimately failed to do so.
By the time I visited the neurologist, I had already ordered RC seeds. So, I decided to postpone jumping through all those hoops until later to prepare for the next cycle
My busting story:
I ordered RC seeds from
http://dutch-headshop.be
.
Thank you
@Shaun brearley
for sharing this website in another thread. I am sorry if it was originally posted by anyone else
These guys offer various seeds and mushrooms, but you can’t order the latter to Portugal since mushrooms are illegal here. You can still order the seeds, spores, and growing kits, though.
Keep in mind that the website only accepts bank transfers, which means you need to allow 2–3 days for the payment to be processed. I received my seeds in 8 days—3 days for payment processing and 5 days for delivery.
The first treatment:
I waited 5 days after taking my last triptan. I was lucky enough to have relatively mild attacks (as far as CH goes), so I managed without triptans and oxygen
I followed
@MoxieGirl
's recipe you can find here
https://clusterbusters.org/forums/topic/2353-moxiegirls-seed-recipe/
On Monday, I took one package of RC seeds, which contained 40 seeds. After doing the floating test, I found only 3 bad seeds. I then ground, soaked, and drank the concoction made from the remaining 37 seeds.
I didn't have a trip or any side effects whatsoever.
On Tuesday, I had a slapback. It wasn't a full-blown attack, so it was manageable without taking a painkiller or oxygen, however, it was sharp

Hellow to you all,
Since reading here on this forum about your successful stories and all the information that came with was very helpful to me, I want to return the favor and tell you mine.
I am 37 years old, live in germany and was first hit by the beast in my early twentys. After erratic outbursts in the beginning year, it settled at a 2 year cycle always starting in late autumn or winter, lasting 8 weeks with 3-4 attacks per 24h. (reading a while an this board, I now consider myself pretty lucky)
Every cycle i would go to a different doktor or neurologist and was sent home with various migraine pills. When I mentioned CH as a possibility, I was shut down by the dokters, because that couldnt be because did not show any facial paralysis… wich I actually dont, even at the worst „banging-my-head-against-something-hard-and-cold-pain“. So I went on popping chewed up Sumatriptan-Pills, untill the dokters wouldnt perscribe any more.
In 2022 I finally found a neurologist who`s first guess was CH. In his words: „What else should it be!?“ At that appointment my last cycle was over for about 2 months, so we agreed to get the O2 at the next cycle and maybe try cortisone infusions… the first idiea i liked, the second one not so much. So I startet searching the internet and found this forum. Thank you for all the hope you gave me.
Due to experiences in my younger years, I decided to try MM at the next cycle. And here is how it went so far.
The cycle startet at 28th December 2023. My cycle started very slowly with only 2 hits every 2 days in the first week.
First bust 06th January:
1,5g powderd MM in chocolate… turns out it works like lemon tekk, so it was a bit more intense then I planned. While coming down I got immediatly hit with a really bad slapback lastig 3h and 2h later again same as hard for 2h. The following night was quiet, but untill the next bust i had every night 2-3 attacks. None at daytime, only mild shadows.
Second bust 12th January:
1 g in chocolate. Had bad hadaches (not CH, but from all the sleep deprevation) while busting, felt terrible and the slapbacks came on like the fist time. Again i got hit the following days every night 2-3 times. At that point my spirits startet to leave me and I was questioning the whole process. Again reading all the similar experiences here kept me going.
Third bust 17th January:
1,25g same chocolate… That day was from the beginning out oft he ordinary. Had an strong but clear and short hit in the night and then shortly after getting up. That one was less painfull but would not go away untill noon. Every time i thought it would fade, it ramped up again. In the evening just before I took the MM i felt it coming on again, but it was stopped by the MM.
Coming down I felt the slapback slowly starting to burn in my face, but then fading away… that happend twice or more in the next hour and suddenly I had the strong feeling, that there wont be any more attacks. Like my body knows now how to keep it from getting out

text: Hello everyone.  It has been a long time since i have been on here and i feel a little guilty about that since some people on here have helped me a great deal.   I guess once the beast goes away i try to forget all about it.   So before i ended up using the LSD i had decent success with MM that decreased duration and severity without a doubt.  The problem was whenever i knew i really needed them i could not find any which was the case here.   July 25 of 2018 i was in the same situation and about a week in to my headaches.  Had oxygen but was in a very bad place so willing to try anything.  Never intended on taking lsd but i had a line on it fron someone i trusted so i went for it.   I wish i would have journaled exactly what happened but here is a basic overview.   Took my first dose which i have no way of verifying the amount as they were on Smarties candies.... I know.  It is not usually recommended to trip while you are in an extreme depression but i was desperate. This was also the first time the beast came in the summer so i was concerned about this as well.  My trip ended up being unbeleivable and spent most of it just walking around at night.  I had a profound spiritual experience that i can not put in to words but it changed me without a doubt.  The next day i had a slap back and a minor one the following day.  Waited five days and dosed again however a much smaller dose and had a mild slap back.   This was my last headache to date.  I also took one more after another 5 days just to be sure.  Now you might be saying thats great and it is but i haven't even shared the most amazing part of this yet.  July 25th is also the last day i took a drink.  I had been to treatment numerous times and had no intention of quitting at that moment but i woke with no desire to drink.  My withdrawls were mild but still notable and all this time beer was just sitting in my fridge.  Now i am an alcoholic who drinks every day when i do so this is incredible.  It was also the begining of my eating healthy.  I can't explain this either but woke up the next day and no longer wanted to eat red meat, butter, cheese,  processed food, ect.  And this was literally all that was in my apartment!  I lost over 30 pound in the following 4-5 months.   Whatever happened during that trip has for the most part  warn off and my not drinking and eating healthy takes a lot of work as opposed to the 6 months following my trip where it was automatic.  It still gave me a foundation to start making some big changes.   I still have no idea what happened to me that night. The best way i can describe it is wires in my brain that were crossed became uncrossed.  I wanted to share this because i remember reading stories on here which probably allowed me to be open to the idea of trying.   Obviously everyone has a different reaction to a trip and i had a lot of experience with mm prior.  So i am not recommending this one way or another just thought it was important to share.   Also much res

To my wonderful Cluster Buster Community,
I want to share my success story with busting thanks to the help and advice of many fellow cluster heads, specifically
@xxx
,
@CHfather
,
@Bejeeber
, and
@Shaun brearley
, who took the time to answer my many questions and to guide me in my journey.  Thanks to all of you, I asked my Functional Medicine doctor what she thought of the busting idea (she is an alternative doc who left Mayo to go out on her own), and she referred me to a patient of hers who could provide me with mushrooms. It involved crossing state lines into Michigan from Wisconsin and a 7.5-hour drive each way, but it was well worth it. I only got halfway before I had to stop and get a hotel so I could use my oxygen and get off the road for the 6 attacks I knew I would have before morning. I was able to make it back the next afternoon before the attacks began again. I waited another week to get all of the meds out of my system to do my first bust. I did .4 grams the first time. I could not believe how great I felt. I would not say I was hallucinating but what I didn't expect was that the mushrooms would take away my shadows and general low-grade pain I had between attacks. I felt amazing. I had forgotten what it felt like to not be in pain. I was so relieved I was in tears.
The first night I had 6 attacks, but each night after that, they became less severe and fewer. After the one-week waiting period, I did another bust with .6 grams. Again I felt great. From there, I gradually improved, and within a few days, my shadows were gone, and I was done with my cycle! It felt like a miracle, and I never even had to do a third bust. That was almost 3 months ago, and I am still cluster-free.
I was also able to get extra mushrooms which are dried and frozen, so I know that if a cycle returns, I have what I need to heal myself. That knowledge has made me much less anxious and makes this condition less of a tragedy for me.
I would never have had the knowledge or confidence to do a bust had it not been for this trusted group of amazing people! Thank you from the bottom of my heart. This group is saving lives and is literally the only resource that is truly knowledgable and willing to share their personal experiences and information.
I truly hope that there is legal access to hallucinogenics soon and that the medical community accepts this as a legitimate treatment.
You are my heroes!

My name is Brendan Burns and I have been a cluster sufferer for just about 20 years now.  Today I am here to talk about the research I have come across and the experiences that I have had over the past 6 years treating my headaches with the dissociative hallucinogen, Ketamine Hydrochloride.
First off I figured I would tell you a bit about myself and my situation.  I had my first cluster when I was 17.  I was lucky enough to be correctly diagnosed within the first 4
years and like most people I was prescribed triptan medications. For a while this worked.  Eventually however, the clusters were getting worse and worse and I was needing a constant supply of triptans.  Not long after I had turned 24 a bit of strangeness stepped in--I started having grand mal seizures due to an undiagnosed gluten allergy.  At this time my clusters started getting much worse but now I was also experiencing other headaches that did not always fit the typical cluster headache profile.  In particular many of them would last longer and act more like a migraine, some sort of other TAC or sometimes a severe tension headache.
Eventually I overcame the seizures by changing my diet but the headaches have remained.  I mention all this because although I have been diagnosed with cluster headaches, I also seem to have some other sort of headache that can mimic cluster at times.   Early on it was hard for me to be able to tell the difference but these days I feel I have a handle on what is what a bit better.
I've had a prescription for an intranasal spray for about 6 years now.  Currently I am on 50 mg/ml and receive 100 ml that has to last me 30 days.  I also have to make sure that I give myself at least 5 days of no ketamine every month to ward off against tolerance and addiction.  At different times I have been on less or more of the drug.  When I was first prescribed it I was taking 25 mg/ml but taking it more often.  Eventually I found that I was going through the prescription way too fast with not nearly enough relief and the prescription was doubled to 50 mg/ml.  For quite a while I kept at this dosage and refilled it every 15 days or so, but for me it just ended up being too much too often.  I felt li

What sets the HP Gland to go off its regular schedule from 1x a day?
Barometric Pressure from one of the following  causes but not limited to:
Change in season - lengthening of days Spring to Summer, Summer to Fall, Fall to Winter
Geographical location - by the coast you get lots of low/high pressure changes from storms or marine layer or fog
Weather changes - low pressure from storms (high pressure is usually good as it is stable)
Stress - adds up to pressure in the head which affects the HP
irregular sleeping patterns
stimulants
Why is it important to keep it on a regular schedule?
When it goes off from the body's clock, trouble starts for CH people.
For example let's say I have four CH per day like clockwork at
11am
4pm
11pm
4am
this is HP (Hypothalamus Gland) triggering at the wrong time
Here's what I think is going on...
At 11am the HP is triggered for the second time today. It is off the from the body's clock. They should be in sync going off at the same time. This would be my normal waking time.  We all have one natural rising time, but two different alarms in the form of an HP clock and Body clock. Hopefully they go off once a day, together in sync.
My HP clock is operating too fast
I think that as opposed to a trigger, the headache has come about because the HP clock has been "Speed Up".  The change in seasons confused it and now it is running to fast. HP clock thinks a day is only six hours long instead of 24. It is moving too fast.
Now the HP gland is triggering at 11 rather than at my natural time is because it is off.  In my case it is already on the second alarm in a single day! My body is getting the message to wake up, its the start of the day again. The rest of the body realizes it is already almost mid day. The body clock is working fine. It reacts to the message and says "terrorist" and sends its defenses to attack. Instantly the histamines flow and everything gets worse now. Chemical imbalances in the body. Pressure builds in your sinuses. This sinus pressure puts pressure on and triggers the TG nerve. Now the pain receptors have been flooded by a "key " molecule that fits into them. This molecule we will call K. It is the molecule that fits in the receptor causing a protein P to be released in the brain saying "OW! HOLY HELL THIS HURTS!"
Your body clock does not quit fighting. It continues to send messages and fight the imaginary terrorist trying to wake up the body for no reason.
Your brain is flooded by K everywhere. all your pain receptors are being blown up and sucked out of the protein P.
You feel the headache as long as K is filled and triggering P to your brain.
The good news is P runs out. That is when your headache stops. Some people hold a lot of P some hold a little. This is called tolerance of Pain. you have a  lot of P you will suffer a lot of pain. You have a little P, you will have pain but shorter headaches. I have a lot of P. My headaches can be minimum 2-4 hours and then 2-4 more sitting around as the P leav

After almost 2+ months of some of the worst attacks I've ever experienced I think I may have busted this current cycle .
I'm currently @ 60 hours pf when I've only been able to reach ~20 at most through out this cycle.

I was becoming increasingly concerned I may have been experiencing a TIA or outright stroke because the symptoms were so profound. Hi everyone.
I last posted about a year and a half ago sharing my experiences with DMT as an abortive, thread can be found below. I wanted to log back in and share some experiences of my recent cluster attacks where again DMT has proven to work well, just in case my experiences prove useful for others
https://clusterbusters.org/forums/topic/7444-dmt-first-time-finding-an-effective-abortive/#comment-71740
Ive been waiting 1.5 years to have another cluster to see if DMT would again be effective and a few days ago I had my chance. I woke up at 5am with a small cluster (tried oxygen, seemed to work) but then an hour later at 6am I had a proper attack. I pulled out
the DMT vape
Ive kept in my bedside table, loaded it with wax and had a small amount (as Im not experienced with DMT I was worried about having too much and tripping while in pain). Didnt really feel anything and the cluster persisted. About a minute later I had a larger inhale and this time held. A few seconds after exhaling my fingers and toes started to tingle and the headache pain halved in about 10 seconds. After a minute without any serious tripping I had a 3rd inhale of about the same. The ceiling swam a bit and I saw some fractals and just like that the cluster was gone. Literally in a matter of seconds.
30 minutes later I was driving to work with no discernible lingering of the headache or the DMT. About 2 hours later I experienced a shadow while at work but it didnt evolve into a full blown headache.
The next day I was driving when the headache started around 6:30am so I pulled over, hit the DMT and waited it out. I was a bit too scared of having too much in a semi-public place so I never felt the fingers and toes tingle and it just knocked the headache down a few notches, but was able to get through the day without any of my colleagues being any the wiser to what was happening to me.
I also take verapamil twice a day when my clusters start as that seems to help shorten the period and Ive now been a few days without another attack. I'll stay on the verapamil for a few weeks and Im hoping I'll make it through this period without any further attacks. Im careful to not drink and to try and get as much rest as possible when Im in an attack period as well.
It has made such a difference to my mental state to have an abortive option. I run a small business and work as a contractor, so the fact I was able to still make it to work on time last week, without my client having a clue that I was unwell and would potentially be unreliable as a result, is such a huge win. I'm organising to meet with my cluster-specialising Neurologist to walk him through DMT as my experiences now show some clear data of it working as an abortive.
Feel free to fire off any questions below and Ill answer as best I can. I dont have any idea about actually acquiring DMT unfortunately, it was a gift from a friend who has since passed away so Im just being careful not to use much of what I have.
Cheers
Dan

MUST

I wasn't able to find a huge number of detailed bust reports for LSD, so I wanted to write up a report that was quite thorough, in case it's useful to someone (even if it's just future me!)
Context: I'm 27, male, based in London. This is my third cycle so far, so I'm still very new to ECH. My first two cycles were two years apart. In my first cycle, I received a diagnosis after 6 weeks, found Custerbusters after 8 weeks, and spent the following 2 weeks on a slightly manic LSD binge, after being quite desperate for some relief. The cycle ended after that, unsurprisingly, though I was never sure if it was the L or the cycle ending naturally.
After two years pain free, it returned in 2017. In that cycle, I only had access to MM. It took 5 weeks to break it, but got there in the end. I then had 2 and a half years pain free before my current cycle, which followed a missed maintenance dose and an errant glass of wine in June.
Day 1: My cycle with a relatively manageable K5. I went for a relatively modest ~40ug LSD, mostly sub-hallucinogenic, in accordance with the regimen as it stood in 2015. I experienced mild slapbacks (a pressure building in my forehead) as the effects wore off. In the days that followed, my attacks remained consistent in terms of pain, with frequency staying at/below 1 per day.
Day 5: I got hit with a K8, accompanied by nausea and vomiting. I dosed again afterwards, around ~50ug. When I came down, I was hit with a K8 slapback, and then a K9 the following day. Frequency was now up to around 2-3/day, often with one attack significantly worse than others.
Day 9: Dosed again, this time ~100ug, after coming across more up to date advice which suggested 'recreational doses' for busting with L. I decided to bring the bust forward a day, basically because I had to be functional the next day, but also because in my recreational use I'd found that my tolerance level was usually unnoticeable after 3 days off. Perhaps this was a bad call though, as I got hit with 2 and a half hours of K10, hands down the worst attack of my life. Decided after this that I needed to get hold of some O2, as the caffeine and hot showers weren't particularly helpful anymore.
Day 12: Finally was able to start the D3 regimen. Also started cutting chocolate from my diet and stopped skipping meals, as I began to suspect these were triggers.
Day 16: After several days of battling my doctor for it, I finally got my hands on some O2. Can't believe I went without it for the past 16 days, and the two cycles before this one! I felt giddy with relief after killing my first attack with it, made a mental note to ensure I had it from day 1 of my next cycle.
Day 17: Dosed again with around~100ug LSD, very slapback heavy trip but super manageable with my tank by my side.

21 year Episodic. Clusters used to happen every 9 months, lasted 1 month with 1 or two attack / day lasting 1 hour each. The cycle would be like clockwork, almost to the day. Back then I was treating with maxalt wafers only, and running cold water on my head. Eventually was prescribed amitriptyline, which unsurprisingly didn't do anything. Sometimes my cycle would
end
with a day-long attack. Different from a regular cluster headache, but, still on the cluster side. Really weird. Anyway, I would often end up in the ER with these ending attacks, but, they were always bad news / good news. Bad because I knew they would last at least 24 hours; and were brutal (not as bad as a CH attack, but, pretty unbearable due to the length of the attack and no sleep). Good because I knew the cycle would end after that one weird one. Eventually saw my first neurologist in 2012 after a trip to the ER (10 years after my CHs started). I've had all of the required brain scans, etc. and got the official diagnosis. Neurologist recommended maxalt wafers, home oxygen, prednisone taper at the start of a cycle, and verapamil for the duration of the cycle and for 6 weeks after it clears. Family doctor wouldn't prescribe me any of those things except for Maxalt. Said everything else was too risky.
Found a new Family Doctor 2 years later (2014). Got me hooked up with another Neurologist, and got the same treatment plan recommended. Got all of my scripts filled, etc.
Over the years, my cycles spread out longer (longer remission), but, more attacks while in cycle. One cycle, I didn't realize the risks of too many triptans (was taking 2, 3, sometimes 4 per day that cycle). Ended up getting really bad rebound headaches. Cycle lasted much longer, and I ended up getting upwards of 8 attacks per day. After that cycle, and realizing what had happened; I've tried to avoid the triptans whenever possible. I rely on home o2, and only use a maxalt nasal spray if I'm out and don't have access to o2 or any other options.

5) Still doing the D3 regimen
6) Have tried adding melatonin in before bed (10mg)
7) Energy drinks to try to help clear shadows
8) Ice-pack on the head for shadows, and sometimes during attacks (other times I can't stand it)
9) No alcohol

Hello Everyone, this is my first post although I've cruised around on the board for years. CB has been so extremely helpful to me...especially in helping me keep my sanity...thanks to you all (Batch, Psiloscribe, Ricardo...so many others). This is the kindest forum I have ever visited on the internet and I could go on for hours about that. But, I want to get to the point and maybe try to help, If I can. I believe a quick introduction is in order.
I'm a 57 year old male living in Oregon. I developed cluster headaches in a traditional way. Around age 38 or so I started to have headaches in the evening. At first they seemed like a sinus headache. After they occurred for a while I noted the unilateral pain and associated parasympathetic symptoms. Fortunately, this was at a time when information was beginning to be readily available in the internet and I had a general mistrust of mainstream medicine, so I avoided the usual misdiagnoses by resorting to self-diagnosis via the internet. My symptoms were a perfect match. I've since had a MRI which failed to show any other etiology for the pain. My cycles were at first more predictable as to seasons for the cycle and times for the attacks. Over time they both became more unpredictable. After maybe 7 years, the headaches switched sides, so, presently, I might go into cycle on one side or the other with no apparent rhyme or reason.
After diagnosis, I searched for something to help (the prognosis, traditionally, is so bleak it was depressing and I refused to accept in my mind). Around the time MM were first being more broadly discussed on the internet and, although I was in disbelief that THIS was the cure, I quickly gave it a try and was amazed at the results. They worked, well...like magic at first, then, over time, the magic wore off a bit but I still found them very helpful and do to this day. Other than that, I rely heavily on O2 during my attacks.  It works great mostly, but not always. One theory I came to believe in was that minimizing the number of attacks in a cycle by whatever means helped reduce future attacks and end the cycle.
A definite change that occurred and seemed to progress as the years went by was that, when in cycle, I was triggered by eating. No just the usual triggers (beer, chocolate, etc.) but just eating in general. I felt my diet was very good. It consisted mostly of home cooked whole foods and top quality organic ingredients. My wife and I have a huge garden and raise chickens and our own meat. Despite the whole foods, my diet also contained a lot of starch (rice, pasta, flour) and also had a weak point in it in that I was way too fond of sugary sweets (it goes back to childhood).
Starting around Thanksgiving, as the weather got bad and we retreated inside, I began to double down on eating, and made it a hobby to cook up all the flour and sugar we had hoarded into a fabulous array of treats. My headaches began. I did the usual, gave up beer, stocked up my O2 tanks, and dosed.

I continued the work while the stinging made me effectively close my left eye. After a while the problem was solved and I thought nothing of it. My left eye was moist and teary.
There was no pain.
Some two weeks later over lunch with a colleague we were having our usual lunch accompanied by a nice bottle of wine.
All of a sudden some 15 minutes into the lunch again three was a sudden tingling behind my left eye, but this time there was a strange jabbing like pain as well. I rubbed my eye and my nose started running.
The pain intensified and I started feeling extremely uncomfortable up to the point where my colleague asked if I was ok.
Fine, I said probably just a cold. Then all of a sudden just a quick as the pain came it went away within seconds.
That was the start of almost two decades of agony, the type of which all of you are more than familiar with.
I'll spare you the details, but some ten years later I lost my marriage, house and job and was contemplating suicide.
Yes, I knew by then exactly what it was and tried to find all sorts of remedies to fight the demon. From oxygen to magic m. Of which the latter brought years of relief.
I stopped drinking alcohol around 2003 and never touched any form again. Pure hell. 4 attacks per night. Brutal. At least two KIP 10's during those times.
Now, why am I here. Well I believe after being free of pain for more than 18 months and I even started socially drinking alcohol again about one year ago without ill effects. No shadows, no nothing. At times do I do feel "something" behind my left eye and I do my exercise and it is gone right away.
All that because I did one simple thing, relaxing the main neck muscle aka the Sternocleidomastoid (SCM). I am not sure WHY it works and it is my theory that this muscle can semi-permanently harden due to stress.
This would indicate that Custer Headaches are a muscular problem not a neurological one. Am I right? It certainly helped me.
The routine is simple and can be tested if you are experiencing shadows right now:
Stand up straight and relax your shoulders as much as possible. Then tilt your head back as much as possible with your eyes wide open. Then SLOWLY turn towards the side that has the pain as far as you can (like looking back and up over your shoulders). Now turn your eyes and look back and up behind you as far as you can.
Stay like this 10 seconds. Relax. Repeat.
Again. Hold it and see if the shadows disappear.
If they do you have a good chance this will abort your next attack. Stay calm during that attack. Relax (yes that's hard) stand up straight, and do the exercise.

I sent this letter to two leading physicians, let s hope they can help
My 56 year old  husband has been suffering with cluster migraines  on and off  for about 10 - 12 years. Every 2 -3 years in the fall he gets a cluster, Oxygen is truly the only method that helps, and that is even hard to get. Insurance won't cover it for use in migraines and I can only get small tanks at $ 22.00 per tank, which lasts for about two to three headaches.
He has seen a neurologist who has prescribed  a steroid dose pack  with no relief,  imitrex which might shorten the duration, but we aren't sure, I read that too much imitrex  may  cause rebound effects with more severe headaches which I think was happening to him.
He has no psychiatric or concerning medical history except for  mild HTN.  He had numerous CT's and MRI's in the early years as well, all which were negative for serious disease.
I was able to secure some " magic mushroom  chocolate"  and with the guidance of the
clusterbusters.com
website we gave it a try and it worked like "magic."
We also  stayed off Imitrex for 5 days before dosing.
He took two "chocolate squares"  ( not sure of the dose of psilocybin in each, but this is what "our friend" usually eats recreationally)   on day one and he  had only one mild headache in the early morning.
so I had him take another 1 chocolate square on day 2, and no headaches for 4 days,
then another minor headache on day 4 , so he took 1 chocolate square last night....   No headache.
I think we will now go with  a 1/2 chocolate square  every 4 days.
We need more research on this wonderful drug.  It works, end of story,  As an RN for 38 years I have never seen anyone suffer so much and gain such relief from psilocybin.   He was becoming depressed, severely depressed and the physical toll during the headaches left him exhausted as well. He was at the end of his rope but now has hope.     He couldn't leave the house in fear of a headache, was afraid to drive, etc.   It is such a terrible condition that few people and physicians understand.
Psilocybin might not be the drug of choice for some patients, but there are other medications that  do not work for some patients as well.  Every drug is contraindicated in one way or another. Psilocybin and  depression was in the news again today., they said 5 % suicidal rate, Well antidepressants we currently prescribe have suicidal risk too.    Cluster headaches alone can cause suicide.
So my main reason for writing .....
Where do we go from here, how do we help these patients?
This is now a major goal of mine.   This information and medication need to be available to these patients.  No one should suffer with these headaches when there is a treatment that works.
I have seen some trials that you were both involved in ,   but what has become of them?   I / we/ other patients  need to get this drug in a legal manner. I am sure you two highly educated physicians can do something.
Please help these patients suffering with cluster migra

Hello everyone, I am sorry I haven’t responded until now but I do want to answer all of your questions. First, let me tell you a little more background about me. I have a degree in Nursing and worked as a Trauma/ER nurse and a Cardiac Catheterization Nurse for over 20 years. When Cluster Headaches came into my life I was a healthy adult with no prior history of any neurological disorders. I was diagnosed and went through all of the testing one would expect, MRI, Labwork etc. everything came back normal. I immediately started trying to find a “cure” for what was the worst pain and subsequent ancillary symptoms, I had ever experienced. I was familiar with the medical system and had many doctors try and help me. I have tried and had some short relief with therapies such as oxygen therapy, medication, Vitamin D therapy and micro-dosing Magic Mushrooms, high dose Botox injections, Lidocaine injections, Hot pepper nasal spray to name most of them. The problem that continued to occur was that I would get some short term relief, only to see the therapy slowly stop working and would go back into a full cycle.
i have had cycles that lasted 7 months and would have 4-8 severe attacks daily with many ancillary symptoms. I was literally barely surviving each horrific day. I was actively suicidal and honestly believed that I would eventually have to end my life to find freedom from the torment.
I found Ketamine and the clinic because I never stopped looking for something to help stop the attacks. I came across an article about Ketamine treatments for Migraines and they mentioned CH in the article as a possible subset that Ketamine could treat.  The Doctor I see has had over 15 years working with Ketamine and treating Depression and  all forms of headache disorders. We often discuss his observations and let me tell you, what he has seen bears shouting from the rooftops. He said to me during my last booster infusion, that he has seen better results treating CH than he does treating Migraines and his observations estimates about a 70-80% effective treatment with remission and with many with long term remissions at that.
As a Nurse, I have looked at CH as both a horrible and completely debilitating diagnosis for me, but also, with the eyes of a medical professional. I have tried to understand what could be an answer as to why this disorder is so under treated and resistant to so many therapies. I believe in my heart that I am not unique and that Ketamine could be a first line therapy that actually has a significant chance at putting many CH sufferers into long term remission. I decided to post about my experience because I have not seen much online or on any medical journals about Ketamine for CH and I cannot understand why this seems like a hidden treatment or a “last line” treatment. It should not be a treatment for those a the end of their rope, as I was. We all know why this is and if you have experienced any of what I have experienced as a patient seeking help

I have battled CH on and off for 13 years
it was only this past year that I realized cluster headaches are in fact a real thing and are in fact what's wrong with me. I have been trying to help myself for so long. treating with every kind of over the counter medication you can think of. always with no success. no real reliable relief. finally this year during my spring cycle things got so bad I wound up in the ER. more than once.  I never would have imagined clusters could do that to me.
that's what it took for me to realize what this was. all my life I thought I was sort of over reacting or being weak about something normal people go through with little trouble. as a kid in school I just had to put my head down and bare it. occasionally I would get some time in the clinic or sent home early. growing up it has always caused trouble with work. I don't like to look like a complainer or weak or someone who makes excuses. so I have always kept my mouth shut and soldiered through what I had to. and when I couldn't bare it, quietly submit to the pain at home before passing out on over the counter pain meds. anything to stop the pain.
I have finally come to terms with living with ch. with that I hope I can be of some help to others who may need it
There are 3 key things I have found to help me through an attack or a cycle
STAY CALM!
I have found that panic can make the pain more severe and during a more severe attack, especially if a remedy fails you, you are more likely to panic. this can quickly become a vicious cycle. stay calm soldier on.
OXYGEN!
high flow oxygen usually counters an attack in about 20 minutes if I catch it at the right time. since Ive accepted my diagnosis from a neurologist I was lucky enough to get oxygen prescribed to me from a doctor at a free clinic. I did this without any insurance and was able to find a local oxygen vendor who also deals with the general public.
MAGIC MUSHROOMS
these are last on the list for a reason. that reason mainly being their legal status. make no mistake these things will change your life. obviously not for kids and use them at your own discretion. that being said I have found incredible relief dosing with MM. sometimes short lived but still better than anything over the counter.
in the past before I knew what I was going through I would try anything and everything to combat an attack. I had a few little rituals and tricks but the main goal was usually to get home or  to a dark room so I could try to relax an eventually go to sleep.

caffeine

bengay

Hi friends. This is going to be quite the wall of text, for a few reasons.
Partly to thoroughly explain my situation. Secondly there may be things in here that can help others.
But mainly, just to get things off my chest, or more aptly, out of my head.
I'll probably do this in 2 parts to break it up. First, I'll describe my background.
44 male from Australia. Suffered from episodic cluster headaches for about 15 years.
As with almost all of us sufferers, I have researched the hell out of this over the years, including here.
My cycle is a pretty standard one : I will get 8 weeks of daily clusters, and then a remission period of 1.5 years.
The first 2-3 cycles, i was pretty unaware. I was younger then, and simply battled through.
My go to was ( and always has ) a boiling hot shower, directly on the head, and rocking back and forward.
They hit like clockwork, once per day at 7-8pm every night, lasting around an hour.
I have a very high pain tolerance, but when these things struck, it was an eye opener.
I wasnt even aware a pain like this could exist. Things i used to think were a 7/10 on the pain scale, suddenly became a 3/10.
Its actually made it hard now, whenever somebody asks what a pain level is for a separate injury, im going off my cluster scale, because i know what a real 10/10 pain is now. My 4/10 pain, is their 8/10 pain. Anyway...
On about the 3rd cycle, it struck while I was out camping. Alone. In the middle of nowhere in the bush.
It was horrific. It was at this time, in desperation, I resorted to something i created out of desperation, called the rocking technique.
What i did, was kneel in the dirt (ground), lean forward and put my head on the ground in my hands.....and just rocked backwards and forwards.....and I counted each rock. As i said, I was desperate, with no other options...
It was during this, that I discovered, although it was very painful, if I kept counting, I never got to 500, before i felt it start to ebb away.
At 1 rock per second roughly, this was around 10 minutes or so.
I'd just completed my first abort! During the next attack, i was able to replicate it again.
Although still suffering up to a 7/10, it felt like a win.
But....
As I have discovered over the years, when you defeat the beast, it seems to piss it off. It tries to claw its way around your methods, or find a new way to punish you. I was about to find this out for the first time.
In the following days, my clusters stopped being like clock work. They started attacking at random times.
Worse still, my one attack a day, had now become 2-3 per day. Since that day, it has never returned to normal during a cycle.
Sometimes i wish i hadnt pissed ithe beast off, and just had the standard 1 per night, at a time I knew.
Regardless, for the remainder of that cycle, i was able to abort about 75% of the attacks using the rocking technique.
A year and a half passed, and the beast was back. Worse still, my rocking technique was far less effective.

I am new here and wanted to share my experience with CH.  This site has truly changed my life and I want to return the favor for anyone that comes along.
I am 40 and have had migraines all of my life.  It runs in my family.  Large doses of caffeine and/or sumatriptan would get rid of these quickly and it didn't happen often.  No biggie - 5 time a year on average.
However, 5 years ago, I started experiencing a type of pain I didn't know even existed.  I have been to the E.R., had many MRI's, visits with headache specialists, neurologists, chiropractors, and nut jobs.  At no point did anyone know what was going on with me.  I was told it was just a migraine.  No sir!! I know what a migraine is.  Those are bad but this...this is worse than anything I can imagine.  This type of pain made me cry in front of my kids...sigh..wish that hadn't happened!  Made me want to physically hurt anyone who spoke to me.  It wasn't until this year that I found this site and wow!  Things have changed.
Year 1
- Started late summer.
- Pain levels of 8-10 ever night at 7pm almost to the dot.
- Could sleep.
- Terrible "shadow" for the entire day.  Repeat cycle.
- Went to doctor after doctor.  Sumatriptan helped reduce most pain 2 or 3 points down.
- During one attack I noticed how stuffy my nose was and that I couldn't breathe.  I was desperate.  Found an old bottle of Afrin and almost emptied the bottle in my nose.  Attack gone in 15 minutes.
- 7 months into it I went to an ENT.  Said I needed surgery.  I did.  Doctor aggressively trimmed down my turbinates. 2 weeks later - all attacks gone.
Year 2
- nada.  Nothing.  Life was awesome.
Year 3
- Year 1 repeated itself but on the other side.  Went back to ENT, had another surgery.  Same results.
Year 4
- nada.  Nothing.  Life was again normal.  Had some attacks that seemed random but mostly triggered by alcohol.  It took me a while to realize that was a trigger.  Saw neuro who said I had CH but he wasn't sure that was even a real thing.  Gave me Sumatriptan.  Whatever.  Attacks were on and off throughout the year and it was bearable.
Year 5 (current)
- started having small attacks after drinking with friends.  I immediately knew what was going down.
- by Mid-August I was having 5 attacks/day. (post wakeup, 3ish, 7ish, 2am waking from sleep.  Pain levels of 6,7,10,8 respectively.  Each one lasting 2-3 hours.  I am not sure life could be worse at the moment.
- by end of August I was taking 5 times the recommended dose of Sumatriptan.  It was enough to keep me sane but I used 30 days worth in 1 week.  Doc refused to refill.
- Desperate, I started searching and found this site.  I can't tell you how many times I read through these forums while pacing through my house.  Called my PCP and demanded an appointment immediately.  They told me it would be 4-5 weeks.  I called again after 2 days and begged.  They saw me the next day.  I asked for o2.  Doc was confused but wrote a script anyway.
Today, I am almost completely pain free f

text:
Hey folks!
First let me say that I'm grateful to find these forums, and the resources in the relevant stickied topics/links already taught me plenty I didn't know about this horrible thing, and some hope to be had in a moment I really, really need.
I thought I'd share my history with CH, since even those who empathize and care for me don't really understand what it's like. I was one of those people that almost never got headaches naturally, and even when I was hungover it was not that common, unless I had a LOT to drink, which predictably became less and less common as I aged. I'm 29 years old and I live in Brazil, and in 2017 I got my first episode of CH, and of course I had no idea what that was, it lasted a week, and came at 3h30 in the morning and went away exactly an hour later, like the world's most brutalizing clockwork. After three days I was absolutely desperate, as anything I'd take had no effect whatsoever, before or during the attack, but I couldn't get an appointment at a neurologist covered by my insurance plan until a week later, so I endured the agony. Fortunately, he did know what CH was, which after a cursory reading of the experiences on this forum seems rather uncommon, but unfortunately he didn't know how to treat it properly. He prescribed Naratriptan each day before bed, and intranasal oxygen at a hospital, but I didn't have time to practice any of it, as it had gone away and I hoped it would never return.
7 months later, in February of 2018, came the second episode, exactly like the first one. The Naratriptan did exactly nothing, and as I lived alone, not in a million years would I be able to get to a hospital by myself, much less in time to be treated for something that lasted an hour. Fuck, I couldn't even get to the bathroom. But despite that, I had one hope: That it would only be six days more of agony, and then hopefully it would leave me alone, and it did. After that I went to another neurologist, and he recommended Sumatriptans, but otherwise mantained the same useless oxygen prescription. A year later in 2019 it came back again, same ol' story, and for the first two days I tried the Sumatriptan pills, which again did nothing, but as I was recently diagnosed with T2 Diabetes, I had a lot of syringes at home, so I bought Sumatriptan vials and started to apply it myself, starting with small doses and adjusted, until I came to the conclusion that 4mg was enough. I'd apply it and smoke enough weed to down an elephant, and I'd get a fast relief and fall asleep soon after, although I'd feel like shit the next day, but hey, it was just for a week and it was lightyears better than going through that agony over and over again, plus I thought that banging my head against the wall couldn't be good for my brain.
And then came this nightmare of a year we just left behind. As it started in April, I was prepared, already had a stash saved, and even though my Diabetes is thankfully on remission and I don't use insulin anymore, I sa

Dear Clusterheads,
The time has come to introduce myself to our beloved clusterheads family.
I was observing You for long time, but now I feel obliged to give my input.
My name is Radoslaw and I'm from Poland, suffering for CH since 2016 (maybe 2015, I don't remember properly). First two years without proper diagnose, but with small 1-2 weeks episodes during autumn each year. In 2018 I had 4 weeks terrible headaches, so I started my journey through neurologists. With help form the web knowledge I made a diagnose which was later confirmed by doctors - cluster headaches. Firstly doctors proposed me verapamil, but after cardiac research they refused to give it to me because of bradicardia ( a lot of wrestling and boxing in the teenage years made my hearth rate about 50-55). I didn't want to take lithium and gabapentin because of side effects ( no other therapy was known here in PL)
In 2019 in June my cluster appeared once again, stronger than before, so I desperately tried to find new solution. I found new doctor ( who isn't neurologist but is a specialist in headaches - brilliant guy !). Together we decided to take a risk and start with verap 480, under direct control of electro-cardio-diagrams. BOOM ! worked perfectly. My hearth rate felt down to 45-50 ( during night even to 40) but I felt good, without side effects, so I decided to continue it. For next 2 years I even forgot that I have this terrible disease ( for my bad...). Unfortunately I didn't stop taking verap after cluster period, but stayed on it for the whole year. In 2021 each attempt to reduce verap caused some shadows and single attacks, so my doctor suspected that I went to chronic version ( today I'm wondering that maybe I'm not chronic, but such long period of verap caused attacks when I tried to stop it - slowly as recommended).
In 2022 in June ( when I was on 480 verap) cluster hit with double power. I increased verap to 960, but it was very risky. It took 4 weeks to stop the cluster but I got also atrial block 1 stage at the end of cycle. I felt really dizzy, so 960 is little too much for me. Since that time, when I was on 480 some single attacks appeared during the year.
Today I'm 34 and I'm really concerned about using verapamil with bradicardia for such a long time. I have concerns wbout hearth pacemaker in the future, so for that reason I decided month ago to stop with verap and to go with regime D and busting with witamin L.

First CH's started November of 2015.  Thought they were a result of a hangover, caffeine withdrawals, or dehydration.  True cycles started to emerge in spring 2016.  Misdiagnosed by ER as Migraine.  Correctly diagnosed as CH later.  Went through the cycle of preventative and abortives.  Benefits were often limited, side effects were intolerable.  Methodically tried elimination diets, med shifts, etc.  Most with some degree of correlation, but none with lasting relief.  Latest preventative was visiting a local chiropractor.  He noted in Chiropractic school, a member of his cohort was a CH suffer and they focused a bit of Chiropractic intervention with success. I gave it a try.  (Will add notes at the bottom on the impact of other meds)
When engaging chiropractor, I chose to not disclose much beyond CH.  He ran an x-ray panel and review with me.  In his review, he commented that the misalignment of C1 to C3, and other issues in the cervical vertebrae and other markers would lead him to believe I had suffered whiplash ~10 years earlier.  I had not disclosed in Dec of 2002, I had a head-on collision that I chose not to pursue medical treatment for.  He proceeded with a 3-month cycle of adjustments, 3x weekly, with a focus on addressing cervical issues.
In the 3 month cycle, which started about 2 weeks after the start of my CH cycle, the headaches stopped almost entirely.  3 months in, I have a few breaks through CHs,  average 1 or fewer per day (which is miraculous as on cycle I am consistently at 3x daily, on a schedule [2:31 am, 6:00 am, and 2:00 pm]).  I've noted that, when I miss the Chiro adjustments (due to work, etc) greater than 4 days, there is a higher potential to suffer more than 1 CH.
As we all know, CH triggers and resolution is mostly about correlation, as causality is not yet well established.  But thus far, chiropractic adjustments, coupled with stretches, shoulder and neck muscle exercises focusing on extension / relaxation, with heat on neck seem to have demonstrated the most effective treatment path for prevention without side effects.
Hope it helps. I am in Beaverton Oregon and see a chiro here.  I can provide a referral if interested.

1 week period with 1 headache (~90 minutes duration 9/10 pain scale)
1 week period with 3 headaches (~90 minutes duration 9/10 pain scale)

1 week period with reduced headaches (less intensity and / or less frequency) (~90 minutes - 3 hours duration 4/5 to 8/9 pain scale)
Back to start

text:
I’m 56 years old, and I suffer from episodic cluster headaches. It took years to get properly diagnosed, and by the time I did, the condition had already taken over large parts of my life. Anyone who has experienced cluster headaches understands that this isn’t just a headache—it’s a cycle of severe, debilitating pain that dictates how you live day to day.
Before I found an effective system, I relied on multiple treatments just to function. I used Rizatriptan, which could take up to an hour to work. Then I moved to Sumatriptan nasal spray, which worked in about 25 minutes, and eventually injectable Sumatriptan, which was faster. I also used high-flow oxygen at home set to 15 liters per minute, and I carried a portable unit at work. Even with all of this, my life revolved around anticipating attacks—how many injections I had left, where I could go, and whether I could even leave the house.
Preventive therapy helped stabilize things. I currently take Verapamil daily and use Emgality monthly. These treatments made a difference, but they didn’t completely free me from the cycles.
After doing a lot of research, I came across information from groups like Clusterbusters and studies being done at Yale. I read about people using psilocybin in different ways—microdosing, moderate dosing, and high-dose “cycle-breaking” approaches. After careful consideration, I chose the high-dose method.
My first experience was a full weekend: 7 grams on Friday, 7 grams on Saturday, and 7 grams on Sunday. What I noticed immediately, the intensity was way less and they started to come back slowly, not immediate. Another surprising change was how my oxygen therapy worked. Before, it would take 15 to 20 minutes to stop an attack. After that weekend, when I have any symptoms at all, oxygen worked in about 5 minutes. That alone was life-changing.
Over the course of a year, I repeated this same three-day protocol two more times, spacing each weekend several months apart. Each time, the result was consistent: the cluster headaches stopped. When they eventually returned, they came back slower, less intense, and much more manageable.
At one point, I stopped my preventive medications to see what would happen. The cluster headaches came back. When I restarted my medications, they went away again. That confirmed for me that my baseline treatment—Verapamil and Emgality—is still essential.
What I’ve learned from this journey is that it’s not one single solution—it’s a combination. Preventive therapy keeps things stable. Acute treatments like oxygen and triptans provide backup when needed. And for me, psilocybin appeared to interrupt the cycle in a way nothing else had.
Today, my life is very different. I’m no longer trapped by my condition the way I once was. I still respect it, I still prepare for it, but I have tools that work—and that has given me a level of control I didn’t think was possible before.
This is my personal experience. It’s not a recommendation or a guideline, but it is

I’ve been an episodic cluster headache sufferer for nearly twenty years now. I am father of three with a fairly stressful job in the tech industry. I usually get a flare up of cluster headaches every 12 to 18 months. They typically last 3 months.
Last winter, the headaches were starting to come on, and I did 4 doses of magic mushrooms one week apart (around 1 gram each time). It did the trick and I experienced nearly four months of completely pain free existence, not even a hint of shadows, which is very unusual for me.
Unfortunately in the spring the headaches came back. I only had 1 gram of mushrooms left and it wasn’t enough. I never experienced a flare up in the spring so I thought I was safe for a while. I was about to make another batch… Lesson learned.
I’m now 3 and a half months into this flare up. It is starting to cool off finally. In the middle of the summer I was at 800 mg of verapamil. The side effects weren’t pleasant. Now I’m down to 480. I plan on going fully verapamil free in the next three weeks. Five days after being off verapamil, I plan on taking 2 grams of mushroom as a precaution, and repeat the dose every two or three weeks for the foreseeable future.

I'm not sure how to start this but here goes nothing. I am a 27 year old female who has been having episodic clusters since my early twenties. They started out short and intense, but would resolve quickly. Then they got longer and longer and more intense and I was having upwards of eight attacks a day, usually every summer. My family and I assumed they were migraines and I would just lock myself away in the coolest places I could find and try to ride them out. Excedrin never worked and I would usually throw it up anyway. I can't keep food down, so when I am in a cluster cycle I end up losing a lot of weight. If I would get an attack at work I would run to the bathroom, shut off the lights and press my head into the nearest cool surface. It was devastating for me when family, friends, and coworkers asked me why I couldn't just work through the pain. I felt weak and pathetic. It wasn't until nursing school that I learned that these were clusters and I felt hope that there was treatment out there for me. When I discovered oxygen therapy I just about cried with relief because I wasn't sure how much longer I could handle it. Since using oxygen I had no idea how I survived going so long without it but eventually, even the oxygen wasn't enough. Sometimes it would work, other times it wouldn't. I tried altering my breathing perfectly only for my cluster to rebound stronger and it crushed me. I was losing the energy to even try to find new ways to breath through my non-rebreather mask for relief. Then came my Imitrex injections which were heaven sent. My NP was unfamiliar with the condition and prescribed me multiple doses. I knew that they don't recommend taking more than for doses in a month period, but I didn't care so I didn't inform her. To me, the relief was worth any risk. Eventually she learned this and called me to inform me that I wasn't supposed to take so many doses, but how could I not. The pain is so extreme that there aren't enough words to describe it and the Imitrex can take it away in seconds. Luckily my remission periods are getting longer, but my clusters are getting less consistent. I now can get them at any season and at any time and they don't seem to fit any pattern. When I am in the middle of a cluster I am constantly praying that God either take the pain away or finally let me die, even though outside of the cluster I still want to live. I'm currently taking a break from nursing and I no idea how the hell I'm supposed maintain that career with such an unreliable condition. I don't have a spouse or children, but my family is aware of my clusters. They are supportive, but when I miss out on events I can tell they are frustrated with me, It's not their fault, but I hate being in public mushing my face against dirty toilets while strangers try to ask if I'm ok. I'm not. I'm tired. I want to be done.

My last cycle was nearly 7 years ago and that was by far the longest period between cycles in over 25 years. I have been in cycle for nearly a month and full blown for nearly two weeks. I believed my last remission was due to the use of melatonin. I took it faithfully for a year and then would resume taking it if I felt like a cycle was starting. I actually thought I had out grown my headaches (I know wishful thinking) until this last cycle. I have oxygen and imitrex (which I hate doing) and am intrigued by the psilocybin approach not to mention desperate. Sorry for the long story but here's my two questions. 1. has anyone else had any success with melatonin. 2. Do I have to quit taking melatonin if I decide to try psilocybin approach. Thanks to anyone that can help.
I have not been back to the site since my first post on the melatonin. Thank you for all who replied. I had been detoxing for five days avoiding everything but oxygen with eager anticipation for trying the vitamin M on the weekend of 9/14/12. Unfortunately I wasn't able to secure any vitamin M and by Monday the 17th I had to succumb to an Imitrex injection as I could no longer fight the beast off with just oxygen. 9/15/12 after fighting the beast all night with oxygen I became so incapacitated that I was unable to load my injector and had to wake my wife to have her inject me with another Imitrex shot. 9/16/12 same pattern fight the beast all night with no relief from oxygen (seems as the the oxygen is feeding the beast now) inject myself with another Imitrex for the first time the headache does not go away and I have to inject with a second Imitrex. Still the beast won't go away, I'm in full panic mode as this had never happened. My poor wife is crying and begging me to go to the hospital. I know this won't matter because I have been down this road before and typically the ER staff is ill equipped and uneducated on how to treat this monster. I go because I am desperate and know that even though I should not take another Imitrex injection I will do just that and figure at least I will be at the hospital and under medical supervision. My wife calls our family doctor on our way to the hospital so he can communicate with the ER staff and prepare them to act quickly (I love my wife immensely) I am quickly admitted and taken into a room and given Dilaudid in the hip and after an IV is hooked up another dose in the IV all the while I am hooked up to oxygen too. My wife explains to the Dr. that I had already taken two shots of Imitrex and didn't want me taking a third shot without being under the supervision of a Dr. if that was even an option. The Dilaudid altered the pain slightly but didn't chase away the demon.
